channel.title: Comments for page "Licensing Guide"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Licensing Guide"

item.title: 2019 Update
item.content:encoded: We've just implemented a major update to make information more clear, provide a new section for specific types of content creators, and overall improve the guide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This means that, Dr. Gears, for example, can sell the copyright to his original idea "Big Lizard That Hates People And Regenerates A Lot."

Coming soon to a theater near you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Requesting that the licensing guide is updated to include the current blurb the licensing team is sending out:

Content relating to the SCP Foundation, including the SCP Foundation logo, is licensed under Creative Commons ShareAlike 3.0 and all concepts originate from http://www.scp-wiki.net.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Image Team is way ahead of you on this- see image use policy and/or contact them for detailed information. They've also been doing a formal review over the past year or so to ensure that all images are properly sourced, attributed, and either CC compliant or otherwise cleared for limited use by the creator in cases like 173.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I will be getting in touch with the licensing team more on this but wanted to ask more about the SCP images being used on the main list and the CC license of the SCP wiki. Currently the license page lists three SCPs (173, 111, 1926) as ones people cannot use the image likeness of due to not being released by the original artist, but can we get a more clear list of what is and is not cleared?

Pages like SCP-066 and SCP-1471 in the comments leave it unclear if the images are released under CC or just being credited to the original artist.

I find it very concerning that these kinds of things are not as cut and dry as they should be. There are a lot of SCP works that are based off of the images shown, but if the image doesn't actually license that at best makes a grey area, at worst sets up an artist to be in the legal wrong.

Personally, I'd like to see the wiki go back and replace all conflicting images with CC cleared ones, even if it means redesigning old SCPs (like a new version of the SCP-173 statue) but I know that's a long shot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Zyn is right that the licensing team is handling such things. They have been getting in contact about any and all SCP related items posted online without proper attribution. It's a very easy license note to add to just about any page or description so it's a good thing everyone should include upfront.

As Scorpion451 points out, everything is legally released under the CC license if it's an SCP thing. If an artist makes an awesome SCP drawing, I can legally upload that drawing to a shirt and sell it as long as I attribute it to them and release it as such. They can also, in turn, upload a shirt with the design too. They can also be hella mad at me for uploading their work, but that is CC and that is fair and legal. No one has to cut the original artist in or has to ask permission first even.

SCP stuff falls under CC licensing and save for a few exceptions (like SCP-173) it's fair game to use within the requirements of the CC license. Of course, there's ethical practices and workarounds here. If you start selling your SCP shirt, you obviously have the design file so unless you post that image, it's a little harder for someone to sell the same shirt.

It's part of the reason why we'll likely never see a big name company pick up the SCP Foundation for a movie/game/series but also the reason why so many of us can join in on this universe and use and add to it so freely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I have in the past considered making some limited production items for sale at anime and sci-fi conventions but want to be on the up-and-up. I've put it off for a while for that exact reason. I guess if I even take the leap, I'll use the link for the licensing experts here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also worth noting that there's also certain ethical obligations tied in here. I'm fine with the artwork I post here automatically being in the creative commons, but if someone were to start making money off my art without cutting me in, I'd figure out some way to release the same thing but undercutting their prices just on general principal. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see a lot of t-shirts, coffee mugs, etc. for sale and I'm curious as to how those are supposed to be handled. Is it sufficient to include a copy of the CC license with proper attribution in the product description, the legal section of the site selling the item, or does it actually need to be on the product itself?

I believe product descriptions need to contain the attribution information, but the licensing team handles contact and takedown reports as necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see that the main page mentions that making derivative works under Creative Commons allows someone to distribute and even sell them as long as they are properly attributed. However, this reads to me as if it specifically refers to written works. What if it's a visual medium such as artwork merchandise? I see a lot of t-shirts, coffee mugs, etc. for sale and I'm curious as to how those are supposed to be handled. Is it sufficient to include a copy of the CC license with proper attribution in the product description, the legal section of the site selling the item, or does it actually need to be on the product itself? I ask this primarily out of curiosity after reading the guidelines and then doing a quick online search to see what showed up. (A lot. A lot showed up.)

item.title: Licensing Team Post
item.content:encoded: After a long wait and life kicking me in the ass, the licensing guide update is live. Please contact a member of the licensing team if you have concerns or questions

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There isn't, but you can license the work itself under CC-BY-SA-NC and then have a separate clause stating that significant derivatives can be commercialized. Or just have your own license entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My quick browse of creativecommons.org says "no". Would be nice, though.

Of course, then we'd get into the quagmire of "can we change the license?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there a CC that allows significantly derivative art to be commercial but reproductions are share and share-alike noncommercial? There really should be, as this doesn't seem like an uncommon desire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just think it is weird that people get upset about it when it is a book (that probably wasn't going to be profitable anyway, IMO) but not a tee-shirt or board game.

For my part, I'd draw a distinction between an item of merchandise which is derivative of my work (eg a shirt or board game, or a charm) and an item which simply sells a copy of my work (as the book in question was going to). The former has the benefit of involving some input on the part of the creator rather than merely monetising my efforts. Obviously I accept that the latter can happen (or I wouldn't write for the site under CCA) but my personal preference is that it doesn't.

And to be clear, if part of my work here was art, I might feel similarly unhappy about a shirt that simply replicated that work for sale!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well the advantage to the current license is that people can make money selling physical stuff and given that we were all personally unlikely to have benefited from merchandizing in any case, I think this is probably a good thing. Helping other creative people make money from art is a worthwhile goal, and it gives us free publicity. I just think it is weird that people get upset about it when it is a book (that probably wasn't going to be profitable anyway, IMO) but not a tee-shirt or board game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By the time we got around to figuring all this shit out it was too late, IIRC.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is legal technically, but it is still morally questionable to directly duplicate and redistribute copies of someone's work without permission.

If we really feel this way why didn't we use an NC license? That way nobody could make any money of of this stuff.

item.title: Licensing Team Post
item.content:encoded: Qptain Nemo is correct here. A broader rework of the guide is underway, but for the time being, I've changed the wording of that section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, Pirating refers to the act of redistributing copies of works without the permission of the creator, either for free or for profit.

You're free to make personal copies of anything- screenshots, the classic 90's example of single-copy "mix tapes" of songs recorded from other cassette tapes for your own use, photocopying a page from a book, recording a movie on your DVR off a commercial-free channel, downloading a video off of youTube, using a picture you found online as your desktop background, etc. What you can't do is redistribute copyrighted works. The license, however, technically allows this.

The catch is that it is still a moral grey area- what we're really doing here is operating on the honor system.

This came up recently, for instance, when someone innocently had the idea of making a print collection of SCPs… and only afterward realized that there could be profits derived from this. In the ensuing discussion, it was decided that legally, they could do this, but because of the ethical concerns, they decided to turn the profits over to the trust of the site, should the idea go forward. They also elected not to include the works of certain authors who requested their work not be included, out of courtesy to their wishes.

so basically,

it appears to be awkwardly shaming people who'd do that as if they would be doing something morally or legally questionable

yes; that is partly the point. It is legal technically, but it is still morally questionable to directly duplicate and redistribute copies of someone's work without permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The word "pirate" should be taken away from the article because it's paradoxically used to refer to the act of legal downloading of a work, while the meaning of the word is unconditionally and universally illegal. Laymen or not, this is plain incorrect and harmfully misleading (because it appears to be awkwardly shaming people who'd do that as if they would be doing something morally or legally questionable). You don't need to use the word "pirate" when you can just say "download for free".

In other words the usage of the word "pirate" in this article is completely meaningless at best and can lead to confusion and wrong assumptions at worst.

item.title: Re: Gamejolt game
item.content:encoded: Licensing team is on the case. Thanks for letting us know.

item.title: Re: Gamejolt game
item.content:encoded:
The "Contact Staff" tab of the Guide for Newbies has some information on which staff you should contact regarding licensing issues.

Alternatively, you can mention the game in the Fan Work subforum, since we do get posts there about others' games from time to time.

item.title: Gamejolt game
item.content:encoded: game on gamejolt that is based off of SCP-354 does not seem to be using the right attribution or thing at all link It does not even mention the wiki at all. Sorry if i posted this at the wrong place, but i feel it needs to be brought to the attention of you guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think 053 did that during their crosstest, and it just wasn't mentioned in the experiment log for some reason. =P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …is it wrong I want to see what "682's Lovely Tea Party" would be like?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There currently isn't a store on-site, and whenever one springs into existence without any human intervention it will be run by a staff member who will be doomed for all eternity to try to keep the various sizes of shirts in existence to…

Where was I? Oh, right.

Onsite, non-sales don't need to worry about this. Offsite sales and non-sales DO. Onsite sales don't exist yet in this reality.

EDIT: Why was this marked as a new message? Mark that anomaly as Euclid and move on, I suppose…

item.title: UPDATE
item.content:encoded: The licensing guide has been updated with new information and a new format. If there are any questions that you have, feel free to address them to me or another member of the Licensing Experts team.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm guessing it's most kosher to put it in a post in the discussion thread if you're using someone else's work in some way

Yep! You can collapsible it if it's a spoiler of some kind, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, looks like you understood what I was very poorly trying to convey. haha

I definitely get giving credit in some way if you're using someone else's scip/referencing their tale, etc. Just did seem odd to give the detailed attribution while on the wiki, which is what sparked my query. I'm guessing it's most kosher to put it in a post in the discussion thread if you're using someone else's work in some way? And of course it's best to talk to that person first to make sure they're okay with it and you don't mess up elements of the character/scip/etc.

I found this page going through the Guide Hub since I hadn't looked at that stuff since right after I joined last year, and there's been a bunch of new stuff added since then, so I'm going through all of it. Thanks for the prompt reply. Love this site.

item.title: Licensing Team Response
item.content:encoded:
I'm reading what you said the same way Zyn did. And, no, since you're creating a work for the SCP Foundation based off another work for the SCP Foundation, there's no need to attribute things. The stuff about attribution applies only to cases where it's derived stuff that's separate from the SCP Foundation.

However, what Zyn said about courtesy does apply.

Hope that this answers your question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I've got it? You're asking if things on the mainsite itself need proper attribution if they reference things that the author didn't write, but are on the site?

Since attribution itself really just requires a link back to the site, it'd be pretty strange to require a link back to the site it's already on. I'll get a Licensing Team member to respond to this, but in the meantime, keep in mind that credit where credit's due is great and courteous, and most authors will link back to an SCP and/or mention it in the discussion page if they write something featuring others' work. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… I think my main question is… Does attribution (such as the examples given in this article) really only apply… or, I guess… need to be explicitly listed… if you're posting stuff off-site?

Maybe a good example is a test log for a SCP. I don't really recall something like the attribution examples on this page being present on those. If it's on this wiki, does just having a tag or backlink or whatever take care of the atrribution requirements?

I really hope I was able to get across what I meant to there. This question is really difficult to word properly… =/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bit of a necropost but pertinent information to your question I feel…

A caveat to what Fortune said: if you created a character in other stories you had written previously, and then you later include your character in stories relating to SCP, this WOULD give people the right to use your character in the context of what was in the content on SCP…however this did not give them the right to use the character in the context of the other stories you had written previously. For all intents and purposes legally, the character on SCP and the character in your other stories would be separate entities.

If people used your character in reference solely to the stories on SCP and attributed/licensed correctly, then they'd be fine. But if they referred to anything you had written elsewhere that didn't reference SCP at all, you could go after them for infringement, because that content was not released under CC. Similarly, since you were the original creator of the character, you would be able to continue to write stories with the character unrelated to SCP without any possibility of infringement.

If you created the character specifically for SCP and then later wrote stories about them……..then you might be able to get away with that still as long as you referred to no SCP materials. Original authorship doesn't go away just because it's posted here first. All that matters there is using anyone else's original materials.

It really is such a big legal mess with technicalities around every corner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's great to hear! Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So long as you only use those elements you created yourself, go ahead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another, hopefully brief question; I get how the Creative Commons liscense works, and I have no problem with it. So when I create and post an article or story here, it becomes a part of the C.C. sandbox, so that anyone could use it the way they see fit, so long as they credit both the SCP Foundation and myself.

But, what if I want to feature one of my SCP characters [minus any connection to the SCP Foundation] in my own series, which is not under the Creative Commons; can I use anything of the SCP that is of my own creation in my series, without having to make my series a part of the Creative Commons as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ohhh, well… guess that's a good enough reason to not produce all my works under creative commons.

Upon further thought, I'm not worried about it. Making money isn't a the major motivation for my writing; I do have plans of hopefully becoming a professional novelist one day… maybe long into the future though, if ever!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Erm… if you're looking to make money off your writing, a collaborative writing site under Creative Commons is not the best place to attempt do so or even begin, most likely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that's cool! And I do seriously need to get started writing in general before I start worrying about marketing or cross-marketing! Ultimately, as long as the Creative Commons doesn't prevent me from making money off of my [hopefully] future concepts, be they related to the SCP or not, than I got no problem letting other people have access to my creations!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really have no problem with other people using or expanding on my ideas, especially around here! And I'm not going to worry about this right now, since at the moment all of my concepts are just that, concepts! I gotta wait until I feel my skills are up to the task before trying to seriously market anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's like this.

If you use material that's under CC, including the setting of the SCP Foundation universe, you must release it under the CC ShareAlike license. That means other people can make their own derivative work based on it, or redistribute it as they see fit. You can make money off of stuff you release, just keep in mind there's nothing to stop someone giving it away for free, or making their own version.

Look, man. You have some ideas that would make good articles? That's great. Just understand that to be able to use them here, with material in the wiki, you have to be willing to let other people play with your creations. If that's a deal-breaker, then I am sorry. You may need to find another place to post them.

I'd figure out which ideas of yours you absolutely want to keep under control, and which you don't mind sharing.

And remember this: Ideas are cheap. Everyone has them. Brilliant ones, silly ones, subtle ones. All the time, people walk around having ideas. If you want your ideas to actually go places, you need to learn to write. This place is good for that.

You can always come up with more ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
would the fact that it's featured in any format on the SCP prevent those things from happening?

No. Someone's actually made an SCP-426 film. We've also gotten several people asking about site permissions for projects, usually students, but things like that do happen. (Although if you have a series that you're working on that you believe is will become successful in the ways you've mentioned, why would you need to incorporate it into the SCP Foundation?)

So if what I'm talking about below doesn't pan out, it won't be a problem, I'll just adapt to the changing circumstances!

With all due respect, it's probably best if you get some successful articles up on the site first before you think too far into what comes after. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually no, I'm not that worried about people re-portraying my concepts inaccurately… mainly that I'm too new to have enough of a following to warrant that! What I'm afraid of is, that because of the creative commons, would my concepts or ideas be unable to grow or expand in the future? Say that the series I'm working on becomes a successful novel series, then gets popular enough to create a television series or comic book, maybe even a movie, would the fact that it's featured in any format on the SCP prevent those things from happening?

And as it turns out, I could still make quite a few SCPs that I'd have no problem becoming fully incorporated into the Foundation! So if what I'm talking about below doesn't pan out, it won't be a problem, I'll just adapt to the changing circumstances!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But I still have plenty of concepts that I won't be using in my own franchise, which would work perfectly for the SCP universe. And, based on the same article I read, content from the SCP Foundation can be marketed fully, meaning that I could create a seperate series set in the SCP, so long as I make sure that I respect the rules and wishes of the creators of the SCP articles and characters. I mean, the way I think this thing works, if a film maker were to come across this site and decide that this stuff would make a good movie, they could do it [assuming they recieved funding from any studio], so long as they respected the wishes of the SCP articles' and stories' creators.

And by that assumption, perhaps elements of the SCP can be incorporated into a pre-existing franchise, without that franchise being directly absorbed into the SCP?

Somebody tell me if I'm going to far with my musings!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That sucks, got so many creations that probably would make great SCPs, but I don't want to lose control over them.

Well, how much would your ideas rely on the SCP Foundation universe to be "great"? We can't change the licensing policy if your worries about "losing control" over your ideas happen to be prevalent over your desire to get them out in the world under the SCP name.

Also, just an aside, I think you're going a little overboard with the idea of "losing control" over your potentially-great SCPs. Is there something in particular you're worried about, more than the usual people portraying your to-be ideas inaccurately?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So far, from what I'm looking at, it doesn't look like I could create my own franchise or series, with characters, settings, and scenarios unrelated to the SCP Foundation, and then feature them here, either directly or in a non-canonical fashion, not without losing creative control over them.

That sucks, got so many creations that probably would make great SCPs, but I don't want to lose control over them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh! Didn't even register that it was down there, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the very very very very bottom of every page:

Unless otherwise stated, the content of this page is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 3.0 License

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there a link to this CC/BY/SA 3.0?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not entirely correct. You can use any character here for anything you'd like to market later. But only if you release that work under the CC/BY/SA 3.0 or compatible license. That means:

  • anyone can reproduce the work in whole or in part for free so long as they credit you.
  • You credit the SCP wiki and your own work on it as the source of the character
  • You allow anyone else to take any part of that story or that character and use it in their own work as they see fit, so long as they also use the CC/BY/SA 3.0 or compatible license.

In short, trying to monetize anything you find on this site or anything that you give to this site is a bad idea. But it is, strictly speaking, possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've got a question; is any character or story that's hosted on this site no longer considered the property of the original creator?

For example, if I created a character, and featured that character in an SCP story posted here, I wouldn't be able to use that character for any unrelated stories I may try to publish and market?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Attribution to the main page is a bit silly, as it is not a work that people would probably care about having attribution for. But, then again if someone's work was really truly derivative of the main page specifically, then I suppose it *is* creative commons still, and thus still *technically* requires attribution.

In that case you should probably go with:

(name of the work here) is based on "scp-wiki.net" by "The Administrator": www.scp-wiki.net

Though the current main page is mostly written by TroyL, alas :P so you might want to mention him too somewhere in there.

But seriously. This is an instance of something that doesn't really matter so much. Our main page is just our news, links and updates, not a work by an author who's going to get upset for failing to credit them. Though admittedly if someone was just claiming our main page as their own then depending on the context we as a site might get irritated about it.

As for articles that are deleted over time - they still need to be referenced. It's not your responsibility to make sure that the original link is still intact, just that you reference where it WAS posted, what it was called, and who it was by.

This is a good example of why it's necessary to include both the title of the work and to credit the author. If an article gets deleted and then replaced by something someone else wrote, then if your attribution includes all the information, someone going to the new page will realize from the title or the author name that this is not the article they are looking for, and that they will have to keep looking deeper to find the original work.

If you have trouble finding the original work to reference, I recommend searching on www.archive.org, which generally has the pertinent information. If you are still having trouble finding the information you need, ask someone on Senior Staff to assist you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could an example of an attribution to just the main page be provided? Currently there are only examples for references specific articles.

Also, I was wondering what might happen to articles that are deleted over time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I know about it. but there are different matters.

Naver users more than 70% of Korean users, and they manipulate search results. Therefore, most of the SCP Korean users will go towards the blog.

As a result, the people most harmed are our site users. Knowing that you cannot understand from the standpoint of you and your site, I think the problem is serious.

Well begun is half done. If you neglect this, this problem will boomerang on you. Even if you do not sue, Minimal action is needed.

item.title: Re: @Dr Bright
item.content:encoded:
Prof Hiro, I really do think we do not really have to care about Dr. Kim anymore. We are the only Korean site linked to the original site. For that host site, ignore it.

Yes, I understand it seems unfair,

and Yes, I know this guy is infringing things.

Seriously, we do not need to think about this site name. We are Korean wiki. The only problem is that he does not attribute to the main site and his users. Simple. And because of that, I warned him several times about this problem. But both the host site and Dr. Kim is still unaware(or just ignoring) of this license problems. Don't worry about it. Even if it seems like the Korean wiki does not like their site, it is apart with their license problem. It's OK. If that causes a problem, that's Dr. Kim's problem. We really don't need to care deeply about that license problem. Just watch, and be aware about it.

item.title: Re: @Dr Bright
item.content:encoded: Honestly I hardly see a threat here, no one's going to mistake him for the real thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um… JOKE? oh… I understand… um… ok.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But but but…

Destruction by group mind would be COOL!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are joking with you. Both Bright and Bland were being non-serious in their replies. We do that a lot here. We do not have an "elite legal team", we just have a few members of Senior Staff who are willing to try to make sure people follow licensing correctly(mainly Sorts, Clef, and me I think…no offense to anyone I'm not thinking of).
